lesson.menu_title: Block 3 -- The Signing: Reading the Agreement Before You Sign
lesson.menu_desc: The deal is agreed and the agreement is drafted -- now read it before you sign. Learn the language of a contract (clause, provision, liability, binding) and use defining relative clauses to pinpoint exactly which part does what -- "the clause that limits liability" -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Block 3 -- The Signing: Reading the Agreement
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Vinicius Vale Bassan | Aula 18 -- The Signing

listenings.text: Carlos, the deal is agreed and legal has drafted the agreement. Before you sign, let me walk you through how to read it like an executive, not a lawyer. First, find the clause that matters most. A clause is one numbered section of the contract, and the clause that limits liability is the one you read first. Liability is who pays if something goes wrong, so the provision which caps it protects the company. Second, check what is binding. A binding agreement is one you cannot simply ignore later, so any provision that is binding deserves a careful read. Third, look for the conditions. The provision that stipulates the closing date is the one that sets the timeline, and the clause which stipulates the payment terms tells you when money moves. Stipulate just means to state clearly as a requirement. Fourth, watch what you give up. A clause that asks you to waive a right is a clause that removes a protection, so flag any provision which makes you waive something. Fifth, ask how it is enforced. The party that enforces the agreement is the one who can act if the other side does not perform, and a breach is when someone breaks the terms. So the question is always: who enforces this, and what happens on a breach? Read clause by clause, find the provision that matters, and never sign a binding agreement you have not understood. That is how a senior executive reads before signing.

Hi Carlos, it is Diana, calling about the agreement that legal sent over this morning. I read it before our meeting, and I want to flag the parts that matter so you are ready to sign. First, the good news. The clause that limits liability is well drafted -- the provision which caps our exposure is exactly what we asked for, so that one is fine. Second, the part to question. There is a provision that stipulates a very tight closing date, and the clause which sets the payment terms is tied to it. If that date is binding, we need to confirm legal is comfortable, because a breach on timing would be expensive. Third, one thing to push on. Section nine has a clause that asks us to waive a right to renegotiate, and I do not love any provision that removes a protection like that. Ask the lawyer who drafted it whether we can soften it. Fourth, on enforcement. The agreement is clearly binding, and the party that enforces it is the buyer, so make sure our obligations are ones we can actually meet. Do not sign anything you have not read line by line. Call the counsel that handled the draft, confirm the liability cap and the closing date, and push back on the waiver clause. Then we are ready. Strong work getting us here, Carlos. Talk soon.
